exemplar.title: How effective was the Kenyan government's excise duty tax on cigarettes from 2019 to 2023 in influencing the economic behavior of consumers and mitigating negative externality on public health in Kenya?

detailedExemplarData.title: How effective was the Kenyan government's excise duty tax on cigarettes from 2019 to 2023 in influencing the economic behavior of consumers and mitigating negative externality on public health in Kenya?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused and sustained throughout the essay
Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and kept central to all sections
Methodology employs an appropriate range of secondary and primary data sources
weaknesses: Hypothesis statement lacks precise, measurable indicators
Sampling procedures and reliability checks for the primary survey are under-developed
Connections between each dataset and the research question could be more explicitly signposted
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation opens with a precise research question that is revisited in the introduction, analysis and conclusion, reflecting a sustained line of inquiry. The topic is narrowly scoped and well justified for an EEâlength study, and the mix of official statistics with a small primary survey demonstrates an informed methodological palette. Minor lapses in hypothesis formulation and methodological detail prevent a perfect mark but overall the work sits firmly in the upper half of the 5â6 band.

strengths: Clear and coherent explanation of key economic concepts (externalities, PED, welfare loss)
Relevant secondary data are applied directly to build tables, graphs and elasticities
Terminology is accurate and consistently used throughout
weaknesses: A few technical slips in diagram labelling (e.g. MSC vs. MPC)
Some data presentations (e.g. healthcare-expenditure table) are described rather than fully analysed
Occasional reliance on non-academic web notes limits the sophistication of theoretical discussion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates a sound grasp of core economic theory, applies reputable source material to quantify externalities and price elasticity, and uses disciplinary terminology with consistency. However, minor inaccuracies in diagramming and occasional superficial data integration limit the work to the mid 3â4 band.

strengths: Research draws consistently on appropriate and relevant official statistics and primary survey data
Analysis is thorough, with well-supported numerical calculations (PED, tax incidence) feeding clear conclusions
Argument is well structured and includes critical evaluation of policy limitations (illicit trade, unintended effects)
weaknesses: Evaluation sometimes remains descriptive rather than fully critical in weighing alternative explanations
Key assumptions (e.g. attribution of health-cost changes) are not rigorously tested against confounding factors
Depth of sensitivity analysis or supplementary datasets is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work compiles a substantial body of relevant research, applies economic tools adeptly and builds a well-structured, critical argument that remains tightly connected to the research question. While occasional descriptive passages and untested assumptions prevent a flawless 12/12, the overall level of critical engagement, synthesis and evaluation justifies a high 10/12 in the 10â12 band.

strengths: Overall structure follows Extended Essay conventions with a clear title page, contents, analysis, conclusion and references
Layout, formatting of tables/figures and citation style are consistently applied
Page numbering, word count and section headings are all present and correct
weaknesses: Occasional figure captions are embedded with source information rather than standardized referencing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite a few small proofreading oversights in section numbering and caption formatting, the reportâs structure and presentation fully meet the topâband descriptors. The conventions of an Extended Essay are adhered to throughout, warranting the maximum 4/4 for Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Research question is precise and embedded throughout.** The question is presented verbatim on the title page and revisited in the introduction, analysis and conclusion, demonstrating a sustained line of inquiry.  
â¢ **Focus retained.** Data selection, theoretical discussion and evaluation are consistently linked back to the effects of excise duty on demand and externalities, showing effective integration of the RQ with the discussion.  
â¢ **Slight limitations.** Although clear, the question could have been tightened by defining measurable indicators of âeffectivenessâ; nevertheless, clarity and focus are strong enough for the top band, meriting a score of 5 rather than 6.

**Clear, explicit communication of topic and purpose.** The title, abstract and opening paragraphs unambiguously state that the essay evaluates the 2019-23 Kenyan excise duty on cigarettes and its effects on consumer behaviour and public-health externalities.  
â¢ **Appropriate and focused scope.** The student limits the investigation to a four-year window, a single country and two clearly specified dimensions (economic behaviour and health externalities). This focus is suitable for an EE-length study in Economics.  
â¢ **Minor reservations.** Occasional repetition of aims (e.g., between Introduction and Scope) and an under-developed hypothesis statement prevent a flawless performance, but overall the communication and suitability of the topic meet the descriptors for the 5-6 band.

**Mostly relevant use of evidence.** Government finance acts, KNBS statistics, WHO tobacco data and price series are applied directly to build tables, graphs and PED estimates that feed the analysis.  
â¢ **Sound but occasionally superficial integration.** Data are described and inserted into economic diagrams; however, some figures (e.g., healthcare-expenditure table) are not fully analysed, and causality between tax changes and health outcomes is asserted rather than rigorously demonstrated.  
â¢ **Minor inconsistencies.** Calculations (e.g., %âQD) contain arithmetic slips and some sources (e.g., tutor-website diagrams) add limited academic weight. Overall application is âmostlyâ appropriate, consistent with the 3-4 band (score 4).

**Overall structure mirrors EE conventions.** Title page, abstract elements, table of contents, introduction, analysis, conclusion, evaluation, references and appendices are present and largely in logical order.  
â¢ **Some structural weaknesses.** Section numbering is inconsistent (two sections labelled 3.3), and minor duplication (e.g., repeated figure captions) indicates uneven organisation. The flow within Analysis sometimes blurs data presentation and interpretation.  
These issues place the work at the lower end of the 3-4 band; score 3 reflects a structure that is "generally appropriate" but not fully polished.

**Most formatting conventions met.** The essay supplies a word count, states the title and session, includes page-style breaks, tables, figures, and a reference list. Visuals are integrated and captions provided.  
â¢ **Minor lapses.** Page numbers are not clearly evidenced in the extract, some tables overflow pages, and figure sources are occasionally embedded in captions rather than a standard reference list format. These small inconsistencies suggest âsome layout considerations missing or incorrectâ, but the majority are correct, meriting a 3 within the 3-4 band.

**Structured argument that returns to the RQ.** The conclusion synthesises findingsâlegal demand fell, health costs declined, illicit trade roseâand judges the policy a âpartial successâ.  
â¢ **Evidence of evaluation.** The student reflects on survey limitations, acknowledges unintended consequences (illicit market), and suggests complementary measures, demonstrating partial critical evaluation.  
â¢ **Depth could be stronger.** The evaluation remains descriptive at times, lacks quantitative cost-benefit metrics, and does not rigorously weigh alternative explanations. Thus it fulfils âreasoned argument with partially critical evaluationâ, earning 5 in the 4-6 band.

**Reasoned analysis using economic tools.** The student calculates PED, adjusts prices for inflation, and analyses tax incidence with elasticity diagrams. Links are made between data trends (imports, illicit share, health spending) and theoretical expectations.  
â¢ **Partial depth and support.** While several analytical steps are numerically worked out, causality is sometimes asserted rather than tested (e.g., attributing fall in health spending solely to reduced smoking). The survey-based PED is treated as robust despite acknowledged limitations.  
â¢ **Some inconsistencies and untested assumptions.** The essay overlooks potential confounders (COVID-19, broader fiscal changes) and does not quantify the impact of the illicit market on total consumption. The analysis is âclearâ¦with mostly supported conclusionsâ, aligning with the 4-6 band; a mid-high score of 5 is suitable.

**Clear grasp of core economic theory.** The student correctly employs concepts of negative externalities, PED, tax incidence and welfare loss, supported by self-produced (or adapted) diagrams.  
â¢ **Coherence with occasional inaccuracies.** Explanations of MSC, MPB and the role of taxation are generally precise, but a few technical slips appear (e.g., conflating MSC with MPC in diagrams, minor mis-labelling).  
â¢ **Use of sources is partially effective.** Authoritative data are referenced, yet some theoretical discussion relies on secondary school-level web notes. Knowledge is therefore clear but not uniformly sophisticated, fitting the 3-4 band (score 4).

**Substantial body of relevant research.** The essay compiles government statistics, international databases, firm data and a primary consumer survey, aligning them to the RQ. Most sources directly elucidate price, consumption or health externalities, demonstrating clear relevance.  
â¢ **Evidence of selection and synthesis.** Data are organised into coherent tables/figures that feed the analysis; contrasting legal vs illicit markets and import-export flows shows thoughtful triangulation.  
â¢ **Limitations acknowledged but not fully resolved.** Sample size and potential biases in primary data are recognised, yet alternative or supplementary datasets (e.g., household survey microdata) are not pursued. The majority of research is appropriate and relevant, consistent with the 7-9 band; a baseline score of 7 is warranted given some gaps in depth and rigour.

**Range of methods/sources is generally relevant but with gaps.** The essay draws on secondary statistics (KNBS, WHO, World Bank, Euromonitor), official reports and a small primary survey. These choices show some informed selection aligned with the RQ.  
â¢ **Partial completeness.** While the sources cover prices, consumption, health costs and trade data, the four-year window limits time-series robustness, and reliance on a 28-respondent convenience survey for PED is a methodological weakness acknowledged by the student. Access problems mean healthcare external-cost data are taken largely at face value.  
â¢ **Appropriateness evident but not consistently justified.** The rationale for each dataset is stated, yet sampling procedures, reliability checks and potential biases are insufficiently detailed. This matches the 3-4 band, top of range (score 4).

**Terminology is accurate and consistently employed.** Terms such as negative externality, PED, welfare loss, tax incidence, deadweight loss and price inelasticity are used correctly throughout.  
â¢ **Conceptual application enhances clarity.** Diagrams are labelled with MSB, MPB, MSC, PED calculations and tax burdens, showing confident handling of disciplinary language.  
â¢ **Minor issues prevent full marks.** Occasional typographical errors (e.g., duplicate MSC labels) and some awkward phrasing slightly distract, but overall usage meets the expectations of the 5-6 band; a score of 5 reflects strong but not flawless consistency.

annotations.comment: The title is prominently stated at the start of the report, satisfying the requirement for clear identification of the investigation and demonstrating adherence to Formatting conventions.
The research question is clearly articulated on the title page and frames the investigation from the outset, providing strong focus for subsequent analysis.
The table of contents contains duplicate section numbering for â3.3,â which may confuse the reader and indicates a need for more careful structural proofreading.
Primary price data from a single tobacco firm risks bias; triangulating with independent market data would strengthen the analysis.
A drop in healthcare expenditure could reflect budget cuts or reporting changes rather than a direct decline in externalities; alternative explanations should be evaluated.
The introduction provides extensive background but does not explicitly connect these data to the specific research question; adding transitional statements would improve cohesion.
The student applies reputable sources to quantify the scale of negative externalities, demonstrating solid Knowledge and Understanding of public health impacts.
The hypothesis statement is cut off mid-sentence and lacks specificity; it should define measurable outcomes to guide the investigation clearly.
The methodology section outlines data sources but fails to detail sampling procedures, reliability checks, or potential biases in the 28-respondent survey.
The discussion of source selection omits critical evaluation of data limitations and alternative datasets, limiting the depth of research quality.
Figure 1âs caption equates MSC with MPC without clarifying cost distinctions, leading to conceptual confusion in the externality diagram.
The theoretical explanation precisely identifies equilibrium points at MSB=MSC and MPB=MPC, demonstrating accurate use of economic concepts.
Mathematical expressions are rendered in HTML rather than LaTeX; wrapping equations in $$...$$ will improve consistency and clarity.
The Data section introduces factors without previewing how each dataset will directly link back to the research question in the analysis.
The section clearly defines PED and its significance to policy effectiveness, showing solid discipline-specific terminology.
The PED formula is not formatted in LaTeX; enclosing it in $$PED = \frac{\%\Delta QD}{\%\Delta P}$$ would adhere to mathematical Presentation standards.
Relying on a convenience sample of 28 smokers substantially limits the reliability of the PED estimate; broader datasets or sensitivity analysis are needed.
The deflator formula is correctly derived, showing clear application of inflation adjustment techniques.
The explanatory text for the real price computation is incomplete; explicitly articulate the calculation steps for clarity.
The reference list inconsistently applies citation formats and in-text linking; standardize entries to a single style guide for clarity and professionalism.
Net imports are negative because exports exceed imports; clarify this interpretation to avoid misreading as domestic consumption data.
Describing illicit market growth as a percentage rather than a percentage point increase can mislead; state the change as âincrease of 35.8 percentage points.â
Figure 4 compellingly visualizes the distribution of tax burden, effectively using color and area to distinguish consumer versus producer incidence.
Attributing reduced health spending solely to lower smoking ignores broader fiscal factors; include sensitivity checks or other control variables.
The evaluation reiterates survey limitations but lacks concrete proposals for methodological improvement, such as expanded sampling frames or mixed methods.
